g.data-narration: Welcome to Decision Trees - a powerful supervised learning algorithm that creates interpretable tree-like models for classification and regression.
g.text: Supervised Learning Algorithm • Interpretable • Foundation for Ensemble Methods

g.data-narration: Decision trees learn simple decision rules from features to make predictions, creating tree-like models where each branch represents an outcome.
g.text: • Supervised learning algorithm that learns decision rules from features
• Creates tree-like model: internal nodes test features, branches show outcomes
• Leaf nodes contain final predictions (class labels or continuous values)

g.data-narration: The tree structure has a root node representing all data, internal nodes testing features, branches showing outcomes, and leaf nodes with predictions.
Decision trees handle two types of problems: Classification trees predict categorical class labels, while Regression trees predict continuous values.
Gini Impurity measures the probability of misclassification. For binary classification, it ranges from 0 (pure) to 0.5 (maximum impurity).
g.text: Where pᵢ = probability of class i in the node
Range: [0, 0.5] for binary classification • 0 = pure node

g.data-narration: Entropy measures disorder in a node using information theory. Maximum entropy occurs when classes are equally distributed.
g.text: Range: [0, 1] for binary classification • 0 = pure node

g.data-narration: Information Gain measures impurity reduction from splitting. We choose splits that maximize information gain - the difference between parent and weighted children entropy.
g.text: Select feature that provides Maximum Information Gain

g.data-narration: The ID3/CART algorithm builds trees recursively: start at root, calculate impurity reduction for all features, select best split, then repeat on child nodes.
Gini and Entropy produce similar splits but differ slightly. Gini tends to isolate the most frequent class, while entropy creates more balanced trees.
g.text: • Both Gini and Entropy are concave (bell-shaped)
• Both give similar splits → Gini faster (no log), Entropy for information theory

g.data-narration: For regression trees, we use Mean Squared Error (MSE) to measure impurity. It calculates the average squared difference between predictions and actual values.
g.text: Minimizing MSE in child nodes = finding splits that reduce prediction variance

g.data-narration: Decision trees create axis-parallel rectangular decision regions. Each split adds a horizontal or vertical boundary, creating complex shapes from simple rules.
g.text: • Each split adds a horizontal or vertical line

g.data-narration: Trees easily overfit. Use pre-pruning (early stopping) or post-pruning (grow full tree, then trim) to control complexity and improve generalization.
Training a decision tree takes O(n × d × log(n)) time. Prediction is fast at O(log n) to O(n) depending on tree depth.
Decision trees are interpretable and handle various data types, but prone to overfitting, unstable with data changes, and use greedy locally optimal choices.
g.text: • Unstable (small data changes → big tree changes)
• Biased toward features with more levels

g.data-narration: Decision trees are used in medical diagnosis, credit scoring, fraud detection, and customer segmentation. They form the foundation for powerful ensemble methods like Random Forest and Gradient Boosting.
g.text: KEY: Recursive splitting → max information gain → prune to prevent overfitting → interpretable model
